result: The other night I stood at the kitchen sink washing a single spoon for five full minutes because the running water felt like the only conversation I could bear. Moonlight came through the window and scattered itself across the wet steel, turning the spoon into a tiny, functional mirror. I kept tilting it until the reflection showed not my face but the dark rectangle of the window behind me, as if the world had been flattened and slipped inside the utensil. I thought: people talk about “losing yourself,” but no one mentions how you can misplace entire hours inside a tablespoon. When I finally turned the faucet off, the abrupt silence felt rude, like someone hanging up without saying goodbye. I dried the spoon, set it in the drawer, and realized I had no memory of what I’d eaten with it. That seemed both wasteful and generous—food gone, spoon clean, hunger answered and forgotten. Later, I tried to write this down in the notebook I keep beside the bed, but the pen only produced a faint gray line, its ink sacrificed to grocery lists and anxious doodles. So I whispered the sentence into the dark: “Tonight I baptized a spoon and became its ghost.” The room absorbed the words the way bread soaks up milk, leaving the crust a little heavier, a little sadder. I fell asleep wondering if every object I touch is quietly conducting my funeral, one careless fingerprint at a time. In the morning the spoon was gone—already back to stirring someone else’s coffee, carrying tiny reflective funerals across the galaxy of a cup.

When I finally turned the faucet off, the abrupt silence felt rude, like someone hanging up without saying goodbye. I dried the spoon, set it in the drawer, and realized I had no memory of what I’d eaten with it. That seemed both wasteful and generous—food gone, spoon clean, hunger answered and forgotten. Later, I tried to write this down in the notebook I keep beside the bed, but the pen only produced a faint gray line, its ink sacrificed to grocery lists and anxious doodles. So I whispered the sentence into the dark: “Tonight I baptized a spoon and became its ghost.” The room absorbed the words the way bread soaks up milk, leaving the crust a little heavier, a little sadder. prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
